title: Three ways to find comfort with the Bell proof and the results of the Bell experiments
authors: Richard D Gill, Inge S. Helland, Bart Jongejan
abstract: Bell's theorem states that no description of a Bell experiment can be simultaneously local, realistic in the sense of counterfactual definiteness, and free of conspiracy between settings and hidden state. The recent generation of experiments has confirmed the predicted violation of the CHSH inequality, so one of the assumptions must be abandoned. Which one, and how one reconstructs a coherent worldview after doing so, is a question on which many authors disagree. This paper is written by three such authors. All three reject both counterfactual definiteness and conspiratorial violation of statistical independence of setting choices and state. After a joint exposition of the classical half of Bell's theorem in the language of Pearl-style causal graphs, a joint summary of the loophole-free experiments, and a joint survey of the recent literature, each author states where they have presently arrived. Gill accepts irreducible and non-local quantum randomness and finds the choice between locality and realism a false dichotomy. In his later works, Bell derives counterfactual definiteness from classical local causality, and that is what has to go. The metaphysical concepts "realism", "locality", "causality" need to be reconsidered. Helland reconstructs the Hilbert-space formalism from a theory of accessible variables, and from this theory he concludes that every observer must be limited in a specific sense. Jongejan proposes a geometric hidden-variable construction in which the degree of violation of the CHSH inequality depends on the number of dimensions of space, Tsirelson's bound corresponding to three dimensions. The authors conclude with a discussion.
summary: This paper explores different philosophical and physical ways to interpret the violation of Bell inequalities. Rather than proposing a single new theory, the authors present three different ways to 'find comfort' by abandoning either locality or realism while maintaining scientific integrity.
main_problem: The paper addresses the fundamental dilemma posed by Bell's theorem: how to reconstruct a coherent worldview following the experimental violation of the CHSH inequality without resorting to unscientific theories like superdeterminism or retrocausality.
main_result: The authors present three distinct theoretical frameworks for interpreting Bell violations: one accepting irreducible randomness, one based on the limitations of accessible variables, and one using a geometric hidden-variable construction.
method: The authors use a dialectic approach, employing Pearl-style causal graphs (DAGs) to establish a shared mathematical foundation before presenting three divergent individual interpretations.
model_or_system: The paper analyzes a Bell-type experiment involving three parties (Alice, Bob, and Carol) and uses models such as the 'Loop of Four' (a 1D geometric construction) and Belavkin-style eventum mechanics.
key_observables: The CHSH parameter (S), expectation values of correlated outcomes, and joint detection probabilities.
important_parameters: The Tsirelson bound, detector efficiency, and the dimensionality of space.
main_assumptions: The authors explicitly reject superdeterminism (violation of statistical independence) and retrocausality, while exploring the abandonment of either locality or realism (counterfactual definiteness).
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the spatio-temporal setup of a Bell trial; Figure 2 presents a Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG) of the causal model; Figure 8 illustrates a 1D 4-loop model for hidden variables.
paper_structure: The paper begins with a shared mathematical exposition of Bell's theorem using causal graphs, followed by a summary of loophole-free experiments, and concludes with three individual sections where each author presents their specific physical interpretation.
why_interesting: It provides a deep foundational analysis of the implications of non-locality and the validity of the 'realism' assumption, which is central to understanding the limits of quantum information processing and the nature of quantum correlations.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: Spatio-temporal disposition of one trial of a Bell experiment. Distance (left to right) is so large that a signal travelling from one side to the other at the speed of light takes longer than the time interval between input and output on each side. One “go = yes” trial has binary inputs and outputs; modelled as random variables A,B,X,Y. (Figure 7 from J.S. Bell (1980), “Bertlmanns socks and the nature of reality”)
Figure 2: Graphical model of one trial of a Bell experiment

Figure 4: Distribution of Eab(XY) for |K × L | = 30 × 30 settings. The distribution of Ea10b(XY) is in red. The distribution of Eab15(XY) is in blue. The expectation value Ea10b15(XY) is in black and its position and height are marked with black triangles. All other 841 expectation values are in grey.
Figure 5: Cumulative distribution functions nH (γ) for several values of n.

Figure 8: Observations by Alice and Bob as explained by a shared hidden parameter. A 4-loop has a perimeter of 4. The curve between the points A (B) and Λ has length λA (λB) and represents the column of green ﬂuid in Alice’s (Bob’s) apparatus. Alice and Bob make the same observation if Λ is in a red sector, different observations if Λ is in a blue sector. The two pairs (8a,8c) and (8b,8d), respectively, illustrate that even if Alice knows her own green ﬂuid level and Bob’s setting, she cannot predict with certainty the green ﬂuid level of Bob. The small black squares are a linear graduation. The separation between two squares is 1/40th of the perimeter.
